event: Early hunter-gatherer peoples migrate to the island of Hispaniola, establishing the first human presence in the region.
Archaic-age Casimiroid peoples from Central America settle the island, leaving stone tool assemblages.
Ceramic-using Saladoid peoples from South America begin migrating into the Greater Antilles via the Lesser Antilles.
The ancestral Taino culture develops on Hispaniola, organized into chiefdoms known as cacicazgos.
Taino society flourishes with established cacicazgos, ceremonial ball courts, and the cultivation of cassava.
Christopher Columbus lands on Hispaniola and establishes the settlement of La Navidad on the island's northern coast.
Columbus returns and founds La Isabela, beginning permanent Spanish colonization of Hispaniola.
Spanish forces crush Taino resistance, and disease and forced labor devastate the indigenous population.
The Treaty of Ryswick formally cedes the western third of Hispaniola to France as the colony of Saint-Domingue.
Saint-Domingue becomes France's wealthiest colony, its sugar and coffee plantations worked by enslaved Africans.
The maroon leader Francois Mackandal is executed after leading a poisoning campaign against slaveholders.
A massive slave uprising erupts in the northern plains, igniting the Haitian Revolution.
French commissioners declare the abolition of slavery in Saint-Domingue amid the revolutionary upheaval.
Toussaint Louverture conquers the whole island and promulgates a constitution making him governor-for-life.
Napoleon dispatches an expedition that captures Toussaint Louverture, who is deported to France.
Revolutionary forces under Jean-Jacques Dessalines defeat the French at the Battle of Vertieres.
Haiti declares independence, becoming the first Black republic and the first nation born of a successful slave revolt.
Emperor Jean-Jacques Dessalines is assassinated, and the country splits into rival northern and southern states.
Henri Christophe declares himself King Henri I in the north and begins building the Citadelle Laferriere.
King Henri Christophe dies, and Jean-Pierre Boyer reunifies northern and southern Haiti.
Boyer's forces occupy the eastern part of Hispaniola, unifying the entire island under Haitian rule.
France recognizes Haitian independence in exchange for a crippling indemnity of 150 million francs.
The eastern part of the island secedes from Haiti to form the independent Dominican Republic.
The United States finally recognizes Haiti's independence during the American Civil War.
United States Marines invade and begin a nearly two-decade military occupation of Haiti.
A US-backed constitution allowing foreign land ownership sparks the Caco peasant rebellion led by Charlemagne Peralte.
The United States ends its military occupation, withdrawing its Marines from Haiti.
Dominican dictator Rafael Trujillo orders the Parsley Massacre, killing thousands of Haitians along the border.
Haiti becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Francois 'Papa Doc' Duvalier wins the presidency and soon establishes a brutal dictatorship.
Duvalier declares himself President-for-Life, ruling through the feared Tonton Macoute militia.
Francois Duvalier dies and is succeeded by his teenage son, Jean-Claude 'Baby Doc' Duvalier.
A popular uprising forces Jean-Claude Duvalier into exile, ending the Duvalier family dynasty.
Jean-Bertrand Aristide wins Haiti's first free and fair democratic presidential election.
A military coup ousts President Aristide, triggering years of repression and international sanctions.
A US-led multinational intervention restores Aristide to the presidency.
Amid an armed rebellion, Aristide leaves the country, and the UN Stabilization Mission (MINUSTAH) is deployed.
A series of devastating hurricanes and tropical storms kills hundreds and worsens food insecurity.
A catastrophic magnitude 7.0 earthquake near Port-au-Prince kills an estimated 200,000 to 300,000 people.
A cholera epidemic introduced by UN peacekeepers spreads nationwide, killing thousands.
Hurricane Matthew strikes the southern peninsula, causing widespread death and destruction.
Mass anti-corruption protests over the PetroCaribe scandal paralyze the country and demand the president's resignation.
President Jovenel Moise is assassinated at his home, and a powerful earthquake kills more than 2,000 in the south.
Escalating gang violence forces Prime Minister Ariel Henry to resign as a transitional council and Kenyan-led security mission deploy.
